title: Compressing Bounded-Round Communication
speaker: Mark Braverman
abstract: In this talk we will present a near-optimal compression scheme for bounded-round randomized 2-party communication protocols. Previously, such a scheme was only known for protocols where the inputs to the parties are independent. The results yield a new optimal direct sum theorem for bounded-round communication. They also reveal a tight connection between the Information Cost of a problem and its amortized Communication Complexity. Joint work with Anup Rao.

proof_stages.label: Communication and information-cost framework
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up two-party deterministic, randomized, public-coin, and distributional communication models, then identifies internal information cost—what the players learn—as the compression target.

proof_stages.label: KL divergence and one-round reduction
proof_stages.summary: Mutual information is expressed as expected KL divergence, and simulating a one-round message is reduced to a correlated sampling problem between Alice's distribution P_x and Bob's prior P_y.

proof_stages.label: Known-prior product-distribution baseline
proof_stages.summary: When the listener's prior P_y is also known to the speaker, public randomness and rejection sampling give a known one-round simulation with communication governed by D(P_x||P_y).

proof_stages.label: Unknown-prior interactive correlated sampling
proof_stages.summary: For the non-product case where Alice does not know Bob's prior, the speaker presents an interactive protocol using universal public samples, scaled histograms, and hash tests to sample with error epsilon using essentially KL divergence plus log(1/epsilon) communication.

proof_stages.label: Round, overhead, and error refinements
proof_stages.summary: The discussion refines the sampling protocol's round complexity and overhead, noting tradeoffs between preserving the leading divergence constant, reducing rounds, and the open issue of zero-error simulation.

proof_stages.label: Bounded-round protocol compression theorem
proof_stages.summary: Iterating the sampling lemma over protocol rounds compresses a k-round protocol of information I_k to communication about I_k plus per-round logarithmic overhead, and the result is compared with previous compression bounds.

proof_stages.label: Direct-sum and amortized communication consequences
proof_stages.summary: Using the direct-sum behavior of information complexity, the compression theorem yields bounded-round direct-sum consequences and shows that information complexity equals amortized communication in the many-copy limit.

proof_stages.label: Complete simulation problem and perfect-compression question
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates a protocol-simulation promise problem as complete for direct-sum compression and closes with the main open problem of whether information equals communication, i.e. whether perfect protocol compression is possible.

examples.name: Equal priors require no communication
examples.note: When P_x and P_y are the same uniform distribution on Q, the players can use shared randomness to sample jointly without any communication, showing the usefulness of public randomness.
examples.engagement_reason: He runs the zero-communication sampling construction for equal priors by having both parties use public randomness to output the first shared sample from Q.
